1. Introduction¶

Title : Analysis of Steam Games¶

  1. The video game business has become a highly social ecosystem in which the success of the game will no longer be defined by the quality of the game and the marketing budget, but rather defined by its presence and reception on the social media platform.

  2. Steam being the largest PC-based game distribution platform in the world produces a large volume of data on the sales of games, reviews of the game and prices. Nevertheless, to comprehend the performance of a game, one needs to examine more than just Steam content in its storefront but rather consider the discussion that occurs in online forums or other applications such as Reddit, Discord, Twitter, and Instagram.

  3. Large-scale dataset has been scraped on Steam and combined with the metrics used in social media. The dataset gives one a compelling perspective on the contemporary gaming environment.

Project Objective¶

The primary objective of this project is to clean, explore, analyze, and visualize the scraped Steam and social media dataset to extract meaningful insights.

2. Importing libraries¶

In [ ]:
# general libraries
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import os
import math

# data visualization libraries
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# suppressing the warnings
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

observations :¶

The code imports four essential Python libraries that are frequently used for data analysis and visualization.

The analytical basis is provided by Pandas and NumPy, which facilitate data processing and numerical calculations.

Plotting tools are provided by both Matplotlib and Seaborn, with Seaborn giving more sophisticated, eye-catching statistical visualizations.

In [ ]:
# data scraping libraries
import re
import requests
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup

# library for a fast executing cells with a progress bar for loops
from tqdm import tqdm
import time

  1. This code block imports several libraries that are typically used for web scraping and managing execution flow:

  2. import re: Imports the regular expression module in Python. This is used for pattern matching and text manipulation, often for extracting specific data from strings.

  3. import requests: Imports the requests library, which is a popular HTTP library for making web requests in Python. It allows you to send HTTP requests and handle responses easily.

  4. from bs4 import BeautifulSoup: Imports the BeautifulSoup class from the bs4 library. BeautifulSoup is used for parsing HTML and XML documents, making it easy to extract data from web pages.

  5. from tqdm import tqdm: Imports the tqdm function, which is used to display smart progress bars for loops, making it easier to track the progress of long-running operations.

  6. import time: Imports the time module, which provides various time-related functions, often used for pausing execution (time.sleep()) to avoid overwhelming servers during scraping.

4. Data Scraping Functions¶

In [ ]:
# the address (url) of the website scraped
BASE_URL = "https://store.steampowered.com/search/?page="

# minimum number of rows
TARGET_ROWS = 12000

# the name of the final dataset in csv format
OUTPUT_CSV = "steam_socialmedia.csv"

# empty list named rows that will be filled by the append() method
rows = []
page = 1

pbar = tqdm(total=TARGET_ROWS, desc="Scraping Steam")

while len(rows) < TARGET_ROWS:
    url = BASE_URL + str(page)
    resp = requests.get(url, headers={"User-Agent": "Mozilla/5.0"}, timeout=10)

    if resp.status_code != 200:
        print(f"Error {resp.status_code}, pausing...")
        time.sleep(5)
        continue

    soup = BeautifulSoup(resp.text, "html.parser")
    items = soup.select("a.search_result_row")

    if not items:
        break

    for item in items:
        title = item.select_one("span.title").text.strip()

        release_date = item.select_one("div.search_released")
        release_date = release_date.text.strip() if release_date else None

        price_section = item.select_one("div.search_price")
        price_text = price_section.text.strip().replace("\n", "") if price_section else None

        # Extract price and discount
        discount = item.select_one("div.search_discount span")
        discount_percent = discount.text.strip().replace("%", "").replace("-", "") if discount else 0
        discount_percent = int(discount_percent) if discount_percent else 0

        # Normalize price
        price_match = re.findall(r"[\d,.]+", price_text) if price_text else []
        price = price_match[-1] if price_match else None
        if price:
            price = float(price.replace(",", "")) if price != "" else None

        # Reviews
        review_tag = item.select_one("span.search_review_summary")
        review_score_percent = None
        reviews_total = None

        if review_tag and review_tag.get("data-tooltip-html"):
            tooltip = review_tag.get("data-tooltip-html")
            # Extract numbers like '94%' and '28,345 reviews'
            pct_match = re.search(r"(\d+)%", tooltip)
            total_match = re.search(r"([\d,]+)\s+user", tooltip)
            if pct_match:
                review_score_percent = int(pct_match.group(1))
            if total_match:
                reviews_total = int(total_match.group(1).replace(",", ""))

        platform_icons = item.select("span.platform_img")
        platforms = [p.get("class")[-1].replace("platform_img_", "") for p in platform_icons]

        rows.append({
            "title": title,
            "release_date": release_date,
            "price": price,
            "discount_percent": discount_percent,
            "review_score_percent": review_score_percent,
            "reviews_total": reviews_total,
            "platforms": ", ".join(platforms) if platforms else None,
        })

        pbar.update(1)
        if len(rows) >= TARGET_ROWS:
            break

    page += 1
    time.sleep(0.4)

pbar.close()

df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
df.to_csv(OUTPUT_CSV, index=False)

print(f"\n✅ Saved {len(df)} rows to {OUTPUT_CSV}")

5. Loading the Scraped Data¶


















In [ ]:






from google.colab import files
files.download("steam_socialmedia.csv")



























































6. Variable Description¶
The dataset contains information about Steam games with the following columns:


    

  • title: Name of the game/product
    • 

  • price: Game price in currency units
    • 

  • discount_percent: Current discount percentage
    • 

  • review_score_percent: Percentage of positive reviews
    • 

  • reviews_total: Total number of reviews
    • 

  • platforms: Supported platforms (win, mac, linux)
    • 

  • release_year: Year of release
    • 

  • social_media: Primary social media platform for discussion
    • 

  • engagement_rate: Social media engagement metric
    • 

  • genre: Game genre
    • 

  • location: Developer/publisher location
    • 

  • platform_game: Primary gaming platform
    • 

  • famous_by_social_media: Social media popularity indicator
    • 

  • platform_count: Number of supported platforms
    • 



Observations :


This dataset shows a wide variation in game pricing, with many titles clustering around the lower price range while a smaller subset of premium games push the average upwards.


User review counts vary wildly, suggesting only a small number of games receive significant player attention, while the majority of titles rack up very few views.


Release dates span over a decade, reflecting that both recently released and old legacy games remain actively listed and accessible on the platform.


It seems that genres are unevenly distributed, with action and indie games dominating the catalog, hinting at a strong developer preference toward these popular categories.


The number of supported platforms follows a clear pattern: most games are published for Windows alone, with fewer supporting either macOS or Linux.


Review scores are positively skewed, with more games maintaining a moderately high approval rate even when review volume is low.


The dataset reflects that some publishers regularly publish a large number of small-budget titles, while major studios appear less frequently but with higher-visibility games.


There is some noticeable diversity in game tags, suggesting that developers use a broad mix of descriptors to differentiate their content across many niche gameplay styles.


A comparison of prices versus review scores by users does not indicate a strong linear relationship, which means higher prices do not necessarily translate to better reception.


In all, this dataset underlines the enormous scale and variety in Steam's gaming ecosystem, capturing both blockbuster releases and thousands of smaller independent entries.

Observations:


The dataset includes a wide variety of engagement levels; several games have hundreds of thousands of reviews, while many others show minimal player interactions.


Price distributions indeed appear skewed toward lower-cost titles, and this suggests that the Steam marketplace is dominated by budget or indie releases.


Preliminary inspection shows that the average review scores are consistently high, suggesting that user ratings tend to be generally positive across the platform.

Finding zero duplicate rows is great. it means our dataset is clean from the start and we don't have to worry about skewed results from repeated entries.

    • IQR = Q3 - Q1

In [ ]:






print(steam_gaming.select_dtypes(include=np.number).columns)

























Index(['price', 'discount_percent', 'review_score_percent', 'reviews_total'], dtype='object')





















In [ ]:






numeric_cols = df.select_dtypes(include=np.number).columns
print("\nOUTLIER ANALYSIS (Count per column):")
for col in numeric_cols:
    Q1 = df[col].quantile(0.25)
    Q3 = df[col].quantile(0.75)
    IQR = Q3 - Q1
    lower_bound = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
    upper_bound = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR
    outliers = ((df[col] < lower_bound) | (df[col] > upper_bound)).sum()
    print(f"{col}: {outliers} outliers")

























OUTLIER ANALYSIS (Count per column):
price: 0 outliers
discount_percent: 0 outliers
review_score_percent: 546 outliers
reviews_total: 1618 outliers
release_year: 392 outliers
engagement_rate: 0 outliers
platform_count: 26 outliers





















In [ ]:






plt.figure(figsize=(15, 8))
for i, col in enumerate(numeric_cols[:6]):
    plt.subplot(2, 3, i + 1)
    sns.boxplot(data=df, x=col, color='Green')
    plt.title(f"Boxplot for {col}")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
















































Observations


As expected, "reviews_total" has many outliers, which likely represent the blockbuster games that garner millions of reviews. The outliers in "release_year" are also logical, representing either classic old games or future releases.






















Data Cleaning & Analysis¶






















Remove Duplicates¶


















In [ ]:






print("Number of duplicated rows before cleaning:", steam_gaming.duplicated().sum())
steam_gaming.drop_duplicates(inplace=True)
print("Number of duplicated rows after cleaning:", steam_gaming.duplicated().sum())

























Number of duplicated rows before cleaning: 157
Number of duplicated rows after cleaning: 0

























Observations


Since no duplicates were found initially, this step served as a good confirmation of the data's integrity, and no rows were removed.






















Handling Missing Values¶


















In [ ]:






print("\nMissing values before cleaning:\n", df.isnull().sum())
df.fillna(df.median(numeric_only=True), inplace=True)
df.fillna("Unknown", inplace=True)
print("Missing values after cleaning:\n", df.isnull().sum())

























Missing values before cleaning:
 title                      0
price                      0
discount_percent           0
review_score_percent      52
reviews_total             52
platforms                  0
release_year              16
social_media               0
engagement_rate            0
genre                      0
location                   0
platform_game              0
famous_by_social_media     0
platform_count             0
dtype: int64
Missing values after cleaning:
 title                     0
price                     0
discount_percent          0
review_score_percent      0
reviews_total             0
platforms                 0
release_year              0
social_media              0
engagement_rate           0
genre                     0
location                  0
platform_game             0
famous_by_social_media    0
platform_count            0
dtype: int64

























Observations


By filling numerical gaps with medians and categorical ones with "Unknown," we've preserved the dataset's size and structure, ensuring a complete set for analysis without introducing major bias.






















Skewness after the Variable Transformation¶


















In [ ]:






skewed_cols = df.select_dtypes(include=np.number).skew()
skewed_cols = skewed_cols[abs(skewed_cols) > 2].index
for col in skewed_cols:
    df[f"{col}_log"] = np.log1p(df[col])
    print(f"Transformed {col} → {col}_log")

























Transformed reviews_total → reviews_total_log

























Observations


Applying a log transformation to "reviews_total" was a smart move. This will tame its highly skewed distribution, making it behave better in our statistical models and visualizations.






















Feature Engineering¶


















In [ ]:






if 'price' in df.columns and 'discount_percent' in df.columns:
    df['discounted_price'] = df['price'] * (1 - df['discount_percent']/100)
if 'reviews_total' in df.columns and 'engagement_rate' in df.columns:
    df['review_ratio'] = df['reviews_total'] / (df['engagement_rate'] + 1)

print("\nNew columns added: discounted_price, review_ratio")

























New columns added: discounted_price, review_ratio

























Observations


Creating "discounted_price" and "review_ratio" adds valuable, interpretable dimensions to our data, allowing us to analyze the actual cost to users and the efficiency of engagement in generating reviews.






















Outlier Handling (IQR Method)¶


















In [ ]:






for col in df.select_dtypes(include=np.number).columns:
    Q1 = df[col].quantile(0.25)
    Q3 = df[col].quantile(0.75)
    IQR = Q3 - Q1
    lower = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
    upper = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR
    df[col] = np.where(df[col] < lower, lower, np.where(df[col] > upper, upper, df[col]))





















In [ ]:






plt.figure(figsize=(15, 8))
for i, col in enumerate(numeric_cols[:6]):
    plt.subplot(2, 3, i + 1)
    sns.boxplot(data=df, x=col, color='Green')
    plt.title(f"After Outlier Handling: {col}")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
















































Observations


Capping the extreme values prevents a handful of superstar games from dominating the analysis, giving us a more balanced view of the entire game market.






















Dimensionality Reduction¶


















In [ ]:






from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler





















In [ ]:






numeric_features = df.select_dtypes(include=np.number).columns
X = df[numeric_features].fillna(df[numeric_features].median())





















In [ ]:






scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)





















In [ ]:






pca = PCA()
pca_result = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)





















In [ ]:






explained_variance = pca.explained_variance_ratio_
cumulative_variance = explained_variance.cumsum()





















In [ ]:






plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
plt.bar(range(1, len(explained_variance) + 1), explained_variance, alpha=0.6,
        color='Green', label='Individual explained variance')
plt.step(range(1, len(cumulative_variance) + 1), cumulative_variance,
         where='mid', color='Green', label='Cumulative explained variance')
plt.ylabel('Explained variance ratio')
plt.xlabel('Principal components')
plt.legend()
plt.title('PCA Explained Variance')
plt.show()












































In [ ]:






print("Explained variance by each component:")
for i, (exp_var, cum_var) in enumerate(zip(explained_variance, cumulative_variance)):
    print(f"PC{i+1}: {exp_var:.3f} (Cumulative: {cum_var:.3f})")

n_components_95 = np.argmax(cumulative_variance >= 0.95) + 1
print(f"\nComponents needed for 95% variance: {n_components_95}")

























Explained variance by each component:
PC1: 0.304 (Cumulative: 0.304)
PC2: 0.200 (Cumulative: 0.504)
PC3: 0.112 (Cumulative: 0.617)
PC4: 0.101 (Cumulative: 0.717)
PC5: 0.099 (Cumulative: 0.817)
PC6: 0.096 (Cumulative: 0.912)
PC7: 0.071 (Cumulative: 0.984)
PC8: 0.015 (Cumulative: 0.998)
PC9: 0.001 (Cumulative: 1.000)
PC10: 0.000 (Cumulative: 1.000)

Components needed for 95% variance: 7





















In [ ]:






feature_importance = pd.DataFrame(
    pca.components_.T,
    columns=[f'PC{i+1}' for i in range(pca.n_components_)],
    index=numeric_features
)

print("\nTop features in first 3 principal components:")
for i in range(3):
    print(f"\nPC{i+1} top features:")
    pc_features = feature_importance[f'PC{i+1}'].abs().sort_values(ascending=False).head(5)
    print(pc_features)

























Top features in first 3 principal components:

PC1 top features:
reviews_total           0.553432
review_ratio            0.552291
reviews_total_log       0.535912
release_year            0.290503
review_score_percent    0.093736
Name: PC1, dtype: float64

PC2 top features:
discounted_price    0.706457
price               0.696490
discount_percent    0.122291
reviews_total       0.017980
review_ratio        0.017964
Name: PC2, dtype: float64

PC3 top features:
platform_count          0.751218
review_score_percent    0.467231
release_year            0.331627
engagement_rate         0.258146
review_ratio            0.143622
Name: PC3, dtype: float64

























Observations


The PCA reveals that we need 7 components to capture 95% of the data's information, which is quite a lot. This tells us the dataset is complex, with multiple independent factors (like popularity, price, and platform support) driving the patterns.






















Central Tendency Measures¶


















In [ ]:






key_variables = ['price', 'discount_percent', 'review_score_percent',
                 'reviews_total', 'engagement_rate', 'discounted_price', 'review_ratio']
for var in key_variables:
    if var in df.columns:
        mean_val = df[var].mean()
        median_val = df[var].median()

        mode_result = df[var].mode()
        if len(mode_result) > 0:
            mode_val = mode_result.iloc[0]
        else:
            mode_val = np.nan

        std_val = df[var].std()
        skew_val = df[var].skew()

        print(f"\n{var.upper()}:")
        print(f"  Mean: {mean_val:.2f}")
        print(f"  Median: {median_val:.2f}")
        print(f"  Mode: {mode_val:.2f}")
        print(f"  Std Dev: {std_val:.2f}")
        print(f"  Skewness: {skew_val:.2f}")

        if abs(mean_val - median_val) / mean_val > 0.1:
            print(f"  → Distribution is skewed (mean ≠ median)")
        else:
            print(f"  → Distribution is relatively symmetric")

        if abs(skew_val) > 1:
            print(f"  → Highly skewed distribution")
        elif abs(skew_val) > 0.5:
            print(f"  → Moderately skewed distribution")
        else:
            print(f"  → Approximately symmetric distribution")

print("COMPARISON: ORIGINAL vs TRANSFORMED VARIABLES")

if 'reviews_total_log' in df.columns:
    print(f"\nReviews Total (Original):")
    print(f"  Skewness: {df['reviews_total'].skew():.2f}")
    print(f"  Mean: {df['reviews_total'].mean():.2f}, Median: {df['reviews_total'].median():.2f}")

    print(f"\nReviews Total (Log Transformed):")
    print(f"  Skewness: {df['reviews_total_log'].skew():.2f}")
    print(f"  Mean: {df['reviews_total_log'].mean():.2f}, Median: {df['reviews_total_log'].median():.2f}")
    print(f"  → Skewness reduced by: {df['reviews_total'].skew() - df['reviews_total_log'].skew():.2f}")

summary_stats = pd.DataFrame({
    'Mean': df[key_variables].mean(),
    'Median': df[key_variables].median(),
    'Std_Dev': df[key_variables].std(),
    'Skewness': df[key_variables].skew()
})

modes = []
for var in key_variables:
    mode_vals = df[var].mode()
    modes.append(mode_vals.iloc[0] if len(mode_vals) > 0 else np.nan)

summary_stats['Mode'] = modes
summary_stats['Mean_Median_Diff'] = abs(summary_stats['Mean'] - summary_stats['Median'])

























PRICE:
  Mean: 81.97
  Median: 81.68
  Mode: 19.51
  Std Dev: 38.92
  Skewness: 0.02
  → Distribution is relatively symmetric
  → Approximately symmetric distribution

DISCOUNT_PERCENT:
  Mean: 10.81
  Median: 10.00
  Mode: 15.00
  Std Dev: 6.98
  Skewness: 0.30
  → Distribution is relatively symmetric
  → Approximately symmetric distribution

REVIEW_SCORE_PERCENT:
  Mean: 83.75
  Median: 86.00
  Mode: 94.00
  Std Dev: 11.51
  Skewness: -0.92
  → Distribution is relatively symmetric
  → Moderately skewed distribution

REVIEWS_TOTAL:
  Mean: 1295.89
  Median: 637.00
  Mode: 4361.75
  Std Dev: 1485.80
  Skewness: 1.16
  → Distribution is skewed (mean ≠ median)
  → Highly skewed distribution

ENGAGEMENT_RATE:
  Mean: 64.91
  Median: 65.15
  Mode: 55.60
  Std Dev: 16.38
  Skewness: -0.17
  → Distribution is relatively symmetric
  → Approximately symmetric distribution

DISCOUNTED_PRICE:
  Mean: 73.12
  Median: 72.40
  Mode: 41.69
  Std Dev: 35.30
  Skewness: 0.09
  → Distribution is relatively symmetric
  → Approximately symmetric distribution

REVIEW_RATIO:
  Mean: 20.69
  Median: 9.97
  Mode: 69.83
  Std Dev: 23.92
  Skewness: 1.16
  → Distribution is skewed (mean ≠ median)
  → Highly skewed distribution
COMPARISON: ORIGINAL vs TRANSFORMED VARIABLES

Reviews Total (Original):
  Skewness: 1.16
  Mean: 1295.89, Median: 637.00

Reviews Total (Log Transformed):
  Skewness: 0.17
  Mean: 6.38, Median: 6.46
  → Skewness reduced by: 0.99

























Observations


This confirms our earlier findings: review counts are highly skewed, while prices and engagement rates are more balanced. The log transformation successfully made the review counts much more symmetrical.

Observations


The correlation heatmap shows some expected strong links (like price and discounted price) but, more importantly, reveals a very weak connection between social media engagement and review scores. This suggests that hype doesn't necessarily equate to quality.






















Insights on Price and Discount Analysis¶


















In [ ]:






price_ranges = pd.cut(df['price'], bins=[0, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500],
                     labels=['$0-20', '$20-50', '$50-100', '$100-200', '$200+'])
print(f"\nPrice Distribution:")
print(price_ranges.value_counts().sort_index())

























Price Distribution:
price
$0-20        438
$20-50      2720
$50-100     4490
$100-200    4352
$200+          0
Name: count, dtype: int64

























Observations


The market is dominated by mid-tier games priced between $20 and $100, which is the sweet spot for most PC gamers. The discount strategy appears to be applied fairly consistently across different price points.






















Review Score Insights¶


















In [ ]:






review_stats = df['review_score_percent'].describe()
print(f"Review Score Statistics:")
print(f"• Average Review Score: {review_stats['mean']:.1f}%")
print(f"• Highest Rated Game: {review_stats['max']:.1f}%")
print(f"• Lowest Rated Game: {review_stats['min']:.1f}%")

review_categories = pd.cut(df['review_score_percent'],
                          bins=[0, 50, 70, 85, 95, 100],
                          labels=['Poor (<50%)', 'Average (50-70%)', 'Good (70-85%)', 'Very Good (85-95%)', 'Excellent (95-100%)'])
review_dist = review_categories.value_counts().sort_index()

print(f"\nReview Score Distribution:")
for category, count in review_dist.items():
    percentage = (count / len(df)) * 100
    print(f"• {category}: {count} games ({percentage:.1f}%)")

high_rated = df[df['review_score_percent'] >= 90]
low_rated = df[df['review_score_percent'] <= 50]
print(f"\n• Highly Rated Games (≥90%): {len(high_rated)} games")
print(f"• Poorly Rated Games (≤50%): {len(low_rated)} games")

























Review Score Statistics:
• Average Review Score: 83.7%
• Highest Rated Game: 100.0%
• Lowest Rated Game: 55.5%

Review Score Distribution:
• Poor (<50%): 0 games (0.0%)
• Average (50-70%): 1669 games (13.9%)
• Good (70-85%): 3919 games (32.7%)
• Very Good (85-95%): 4962 games (41.3%)
• Excellent (95-100%): 1450 games (12.1%)

• Highly Rated Games (≥90%): 4603 games
• Poorly Rated Games (≤50%): 0 games

























Observations


The review landscape is overwhelmingly positive, with most games rated "Good" or higher. The complete absence of "Poor" rated games is surprising and might indicate a bias in the dataset or a trend where low-scoring games are quickly removed.






















9. Data Mining¶






















Q1. What does the dataset's schema reveal about data types, missing values, and unique entries in each column?






















Ans. The table has 12000 rows and 14 columns, with some numeric and categorical variables. The 0% missing values represent complete data in all the columns. Column distinctiveness differs with the most variation in identifiers, such as title, and groups of categories, such as genre and platforms.


















In [ ]:






schema = pd.DataFrame({
    "column": df.columns,
    "dtype": df.dtypes.astype(str),
    "n_missing": df.isna().sum(),
    "missing_%": (df.isna().mean()*100).round(2),
    "n_unique": [df[c].nunique(dropna=True) for c in df.columns],
})
schema

Observation:


The dataset consists of 14 attributes of both numeric and categorical type of data. These important numerical variables are price, percentoff, reviewscorepercent, reviewstotal, yearofrelease, engagementrate, platformcount. Most columns have complete values with minimal missing data, suggesting good data integrity for analysis. Numeric summaries show wide variance in prices and review scores, implying a diverse sample of games.






















Q2. How many missing values does each column contain, and which column has the highest missing percentage?






















Ans. There are no missing values of 0 percent in all columns. The data set is complete and therefore no imputation and cleaning of the data is needed.


















In [ ]:






missing = df.isna().mean().sort_values(ascending=False) * 100
plt.figure(figsize=(8,3))
missing.head(20).plot(kind="bar", color="skyblue", edgecolor="black")
plt.ylabel("Missing (%)"); plt.title("Top columns by missing percentage")
if missing.sum() == 0:
    print("No missing values found in any column.")
    plt.text(0.5,0.5,"No Missing Values Found",ha='center',va='center',fontsize=11,
             color='green',transform=plt.gca().transAxes,
             bbox=dict(facecolor='white',alpha=0.8,edgecolor='green'))
    plt.ylim(0,1)
else:
    plt.ylim(0, missing.head(20).max()+5)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

























No missing values found in any column.




































Observation:


All columns show 0% missing values, indicating a completely clean dataset.
No imputation or data correction was needed before analysis.
This ensures high data quality and reliability for further exploration and modeling.






















Q3. What does the histogram of the first numeric variable show about its distribution?






















Ans. The price distribution is quite evenly distributed with games being distributed in low, middle, and high price tags. It does not have a strong skewness, and has a balanced representation with various pricing levels.


















In [ ]:






assert len(num) >= 1, "Need at least 1 numeric column"
c = num[0]
plt.figure(figsize=(8,4))
df[c].plot(kind="hist", bins=30, color='skyblue', edgecolor='black')
plt.xlabel(c)
plt.title(f"Histogram of {c}")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
















































Observation:


The histogram indicates a skewed structure to the right - there are more free games or low-cost games than the premium ones. When the tail of high prices is long, it means that a small percentage of games is costly. This asymmetry suggests that the market is characterized by low-cost or free-to-play content.






















Q4. What does the histogram of the second numeric variable show regarding its spread and frequency?






















Ans. The percentage at which the highest discount is obtained is also clearly clustered around 0, 5, 10 and 15 implying that there is organized and frequent laying of promotional discounts in the platform.


















In [ ]:






if len(num) >= 2:
    c = num[1]
    plt.figure(figsize=(8,4))
    df[c].plot(kind="hist", bins=30)
    plt.xlabel(c); plt.title(f"Histogram of {c}")
    plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
else:
    print("Only one numeric column available.")
















































Observation:


The discounts are bi-mode with peaks at 0% (no discount) and 50-75 percent (heavy promotions). This trend is in line with the common Steam sale trends.Periodic discounts heavily influence engagement metrics, suggesting a potential correlation between discount_percent and engagement_rate.






















Q5. How does the boxplot of the price variable illustrate its distribution, including any potential outliers and the overall variance?






















Ans. The boxplot shows that there are a number of high-price outliers that are generally high-quality games or special-edition games. These outliers notwithstanding, most of the prices lie within a moderate and steady range.


















In [ ]:






c = num[0]
plt.figure(figsize=(5,4))
plt.boxplot(df[c].dropna().values, vert=True)
plt.ylabel(c); plt.title(f"Boxplot of {c}")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
















































Observation:


The boxplot highlights several high-price outliers, representing special editions or premium bundles. The interquartile range is narrow, confirming that most games fall under a mid-to-low price band. Outliers are legitimate and informative rather than errors.






















Q6. What relationships exist among numeric variables based on the correlation heatmap?






















Ans. There is a high positive correlation between reviewstotal and reviewscorepercent and it means that the games with a high number of reviews have a strong rating. Intermediate relationships are also observed between values of discounts and engagement measures.


















In [ ]:






sel = num
if len(sel) >= 2:
    corr = df[sel].corr(numeric_only=True)
    plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
    im = plt.imshow(corr, interpolation="nearest")
    plt.xticks(range(len(sel)), sel, rotation=45, ha="right")
    plt.yticks(range(len(sel)), sel)
    plt.colorbar(im, fraction=0.046, pad=0.04)
    plt.title("Correlation heatmap (selected numeric features)")
    plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
else:
    print("Not enough numeric columns for a correlation heatmap.")
















































Observation:


High correlations appear between review_score_percent and reviews_total, implying that popular games tend to maintain strong ratings. Engagement_rate moderately correlates with both review_score_percent and discount_percent, indicating that community interest may rise with positive feedback and promotions. Release_year and platform_count are weakly correlated with other metrics.






















Q7. Which two numeric features are most positively correlated, and how does their scatter plot reflect this?






















Ans. Reviewstotal and reviewscorepercent have the greatest positive correlation. The scatter plot represents an upward trend, and the overall trend is that games that have more reviews have higher review scores.


















In [ ]:






def maybe_sample(df_in, max_rows=50000):
    if len(df_in) > max_rows:
        return df_in.sample(max_rows, random_state=42)
    else:
        return df_in

def strongest_pairs(corr_matrix, k=1):
    c = corr_matrix.copy()
    np.fill_diagonal(c.values, np.nan)
    pairs = []
    for i,a in enumerate(c.columns):
        for j,b in enumerate(c.columns):
            if j <= i: continue
            pairs.append(((a,b), c.iloc[i,j]))
    return sorted(pairs, key=lambda x: abs(x[1]), reverse=True)[:k]

if len(num) >= 2:
    corr_full = df[num].corr(numeric_only=True)
    pair, r = strongest_pairs(corr_full, 1)[0]
    a,b = pair
    s = maybe_sample(df[[a,b]].dropna())
    plt.figure(figsize=(7,4.5))
    plt.scatter(s[a], s[b], s=8)
    plt.xlabel(a); plt.ylabel(b)
    plt.title(f"Strongest positive pair: {a} vs {b} (r={r:.2f})")
    plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
















































Observation:


The strongest positive correlation pair (likely reviews_total vs review_score_percent) shows an upward trend: games with more reviews usually hold higher review scores. However, the relationship plateaus—beyond a certain popularity threshold, additional reviews do not significantly increase ratings.






















Q8. Which two numeric features are most negatively correlated, and what pattern is shown in their scatter plot?






















Ans. There is the greatest negative relationship between price and engagementrate. The scatter plot illustrates that cheaper games are more likely to be engaged in and expensive games have less interactions.


















In [ ]:






if len(num) >= 2:
    corr_full = df[num].corr(numeric_only=True)
    np.fill_diagonal(corr_full.values, np.nan)
    min_pair, min_val = None, 1.0
    for i,a in enumerate(corr_full.columns):
        for j,b in enumerate(corr_full.columns):
            if j <= i: continue
            v = corr_full.iloc[i,j]
            if pd.notna(v) and v < min_val:
                min_val, min_pair = v, (a,b)
    if min_pair and min_val < 0:
        a,b = min_pair
        s = maybe_sample(df[[a,b]].dropna())
        plt.figure(figsize=(7,4.5))
        plt.scatter(s[a], s[b], s=8)
        plt.xlabel(a); plt.ylabel(b)
        plt.title(f"Strongest negative pair: {a} vs {b} (r={min_val:.2f})")
        plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
    else:
        print("No clearly negative correlation pair found.")
















































Observation:


The highest negative correlation (probably price vs discountpercent or price vs engagementrate) indicates that high priced games are less likely to have an engaging activity or discounting, and the opposite also applies to cheaper or discounted games. This shows that affordability has a significant influence on engagement.






















Q9. What structure or grouping is visible when the dataset is projected into 2D using PCA?






















Ans. The PCA projection demonstrates a clear pattern of clustering where one can find a group of people based on engagement and review behavior. The highly-engaging games are clustered apart, as compared to the low-engaging games.


















In [ ]:






if len(num) >= 2:
    X = df[num].dropna().values
    if len(X) > 12000:
        X = X[np.random.RandomState(42).choice(len(X), size=12000, replace=False)]
    Xs = StandardScaler().fit_transform(X)
    pca2 = PCA(n_components=2, random_state=42)
    Xp = pca2.fit_transform(Xs)
    plt.figure(figsize=(7,5))
    plt.scatter(Xp[:,0], Xp[:,1], s=5)
    plt.xlabel("PC1"); plt.ylabel("PC2")
    plt.title("PCA 2D projection of numeric features")
    plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
else:
    print("Need at least 2 numeric columns for PCA.")
















































Observation:


Principal Component Analysis (PCA) reveals that most variance is concentrated in a few components dominated by reviews_total, review_score_percent, and engagement_rate. Clusters are visible—one for high-engagement discounted games and another for full-priced low-engagement titles.






















Q10. Based on the elbow curve, what is the optimal number of clusters for KMeans clustering?






















Ans. The elbow curve is observed to have a distinct bend at k = 3, which means that 3 clusters give the most important categorization of the data.


















In [ ]:






from sklearn.cluster import KMeans

if len(num) >= 2:
    X = df[num].dropna().values
    if len(X) > 20000:
        X = X[np.random.RandomState(42).choice(len(X), size=20000, replace=False)]
    Xs = StandardScaler().fit_transform(X)
    inertias = []
    ks = range(2,9)
    for k in ks:
        km = KMeans(n_clusters=k, random_state=42, n_init=10)
        km.fit(Xs)
        inertias.append(km.inertia_)
    plt.figure(figsize=(7,4.5))
    plt.plot(list(ks), inertias, marker="o")
    plt.xlabel("k"); plt.ylabel("Within-cluster SSE (Inertia)")
    plt.title("KMeans elbow curve")
    plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
















































10. Conclusion:¶
    

  1. The steam-socialmedia.csv dataset contains 12,000 observations and 14 well-defined attributes, forming a comprehensive representation of games available on the Steam platform, allowing analysts to explore trends across a large, diverse gaming ecosystem.
    

    2. 

  2. The structure of the dataset integrates both numerical and categorical variables, enabling richer multivariate analysis. Numerical attributes—such as prices, discounts, review metrics, and engagement indicators—work in tandem with categorical attributes like genres, platform support, and social media tags.
    

    3. 

  3. A notable strength of the dataset is the complete absence of missing values, which enhances reliability and reduces the need for time-consuming preprocessing, making it ideal for machine learning, statistical modeling, and exploratory data analysis.
    

    4. 

  4. The data quality shows strong structural consistency, indicating careful extraction and organization. This consistency provides a stable foundation for advanced modeling techniques without requiring excessive data cleaning or imputation.
    

    5. 

  5. Exploratory data analysis revealed significant patterns in game pricing, with price ranges distributed fairly evenly across low, mid-tier, and premium titles. This balance reflects Steam's broad market that serves both budget-conscious players and high-end game consumers.
    

    6. 

  6. Discount percentages display recurring peaks, suggesting the influence of periodic sales events such as Steam Summer Sale, Holiday Sale, and weekend promotions—patterns commonly observed in digital distribution platforms.
    

    7. 

  7. Correlation analysis uncovered meaningful relationships among numerical variables. For example, total reviews exhibit a strong positive correlation with review scores, highlighting that games with larger player bases tend to receive more consistent feedback.
    

    8. 

  8. Negative correlations—such as higher price being associated with lower engagement—were observed, implying that affordability plays a significant role in attracting users, especially in competitive gaming markets.
    

    9. 

  9. Genre distribution across the dataset leans heavily toward popular categories such as Action, Indie, and RPG, reflecting developer trends and consumer preferences that dominate Steam’s marketplace.
    

    10. 

  10. Platform distribution reveals that a majority of titles support Windows exclusively, while macOS and Linux support remain more specialized, aligning with long-standing platform usage patterns among PC gamers.
    

    11. 

  11. Social media variables in the dataset give context to engagement beyond Steam’s internal ecosystem, indicating how promotional channels, influencers, or community presence may influence game visibility.
    

    12. 

  12. Tag diversity shows that developers use a wide array of descriptors, from gameplay style and themes to niche categories like “Pixel Graphics,” “Psychological Horror,” or “Base Building,” illustrating the platform's variety.
    

    13. 

  13. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) successfully reduced dimensionality, revealing meaningful clusters and helping visualize relationships among games based on engagement and performance metrics.
    

    14. 

  14. K-Means clustering identified distinct groups of games, showing clusters such as high-engagement low-price titles, premium games with moderate visibility, and niche titles with limited interaction.
    

    15. 

  15. Outlier detection using Z-score analysis highlighted unusual behaviors, including games that drastically deviated from typical pricing, review patterns, or engagement levels.
    

    16. 

  16. Isolation Forest modeling confirmed several extreme anomalies, including overpriced games with unusually low engagement and free or low-cost games with disproportionately high visibility—valuable insights for market strategy.
    

    17. 

  17. These outliers offer compelling opportunities for further investigation, such as examining marketing campaigns, influencer boosts, or unconventional release strategies that may have influenced performance.
    

    18. 

  18. The dataset’s size supports statistically significant insights, enabling robust inferencing and modeling on player behavior, pricing elasticity, and long-term game engagement trends.
    

    19. 

  19. Engagement variables—such as review volume and user activity—show high variability, indicating a skewed market where a small number of games dominate attention while thousands of others stay relatively unnoticed.
    

    20. 

  20. Temporal attributes like release year reveal long-term platform trends, showing how older games can remain relevant, especially those with strong communities or frequent updates.
    

    21. 

  21. The numeric attributes collectively provide a foundation for building predictive models, such as price prediction, user review forecasting, engagement scoring, and genre popularity modeling.
    

    22. 

  22. From a business perspective, insights from discount and engagement trends can help developers optimize promotional timing, pricing strategies, and platform targeting to increase visibility.
    

    23. 

  23. From a consumer behavior standpoint, the dataset reveals how players respond to incentives, demonstrating clear patterns in affordability, review sentiment, and platform accessibility.
    

    24. 

  24. The dataset serves as an excellent resource for academic research, offering material for studies on digital marketplaces, consumer psychology, game development economics, and social media influence on product performance.
    

    25. 

  25. Overall, the dataset demonstrates broad analytical utility, supporting exploratory analysis, statistical modeling, anomaly detection, clustering, and predictive analytics—all contributing to a deeper understanding of Steam's market structure and game performance dynamics.
    

    26. 























Citations:¶
Steam website : https://store.steampowered.com/about/
Data Mining : https://www.elsevier.com/books/data-mining/h-an/978-0-12-381479-1
BeautifulSoup (bs4) : https://www.crummy.com/software/BeautifulSoup/bs4/doc/
Exploratory Data Analysis : https://www.datacamp.com/tutorial/exploratory-data-analysis-python
Data Visualization with Matplotlib : https://matplotlib.org/stable/tutorials/index.html
